lostDestinations.source: El Dorado News / Encyclopedia of Arkansas: C&S served ELD from 1945, Delta absorbed C&S 1953 and later withdrew
search results: Trans-Texas DC-3/Convair 600 service, last TI Convair 600 left ELD 1977
TX Intl served ELD via regional milk-run routing through 1977 per airport history

note: Airport regained EAS service via Contour Airlines to DFW starting March 2026 after SeaPort Airlines ceased ops Sept 2016; currently active EAS

lostDestinations.source: Hot Springs airport history: Trans-Texas DC-3 'milk run' routing Memphis-Helena-Stuttgart-Pine Bluff-LIT-HOT-Texarkana-Dallas, ended late 1950s
Same Trans-Texas DC-3 milk-run service; service to PBF ceased by late 1950s per historical accounts

note: Pine Bluff had scheduled service via Trans-Texas milk-run routing 1953-~1958; airport operates as general aviation only since then

lostDestinations.source: Drake Field history: Frontier served Fayetteville with nonstop to Harrison through 1970s until deregulation
Frontier Airlines served multi-stop routes through HRO connecting to OKC/Tulsa via 1979 deregulation
Frontier Airlines regional route map included TUL connections through HRO until 1979 deregulation per search results
Frontier/successor carriers served HRO with Little Rock connections; EAS service to LIT confirmed in historical records

note: HRO currently served by Southern Airways Express under EAS to DFW and MEM

lostDestinations.source: Hot Springs airport history: Frontier served HOT with Convair 580/600 nonstop to LIT through deregulation era; TI also served LIT routing
Hot Springs airport history site: Delta used Convair 440 with nonstop to SHV after absorbing C&S 1953, discontinued 1969
Hot Springs airport history: C&S/Delta operated multi-stop service to Chicago via KC/St Louis routing through late 1960s
C&S/Delta route map included New Orleans connections from HOT through late 1960s per airport history site
Hot Springs airport history: Mesa Airlines served HOT Oct 2002-May 2008, operated connections to BNA hub
Hot Springs airport history: Big Sky Airlines served HOT Jan 1999-Sept 2002 with connections via ATL/Memphis

note: HOT is currently EAS-dependent; Southern Airways Express serves DFW and MEM. Airport lost mainline jet service circa 1980 after deregulation.

lostDestinations.source: Encyclopedia of Arkansas Jonesboro Airport entry: TX Intl last Convair 600 left 1976; DFW was a TX Intl destination
Encyclopedia of Arkansas JBR entry: former destinations included El Dorado and DFW via TX Intl service
JBR history: SeaPort Airlines and Air Midwest operated EAS service including MEM connections before 2016 SeaPort bankruptcy
JBR had EAS connections to Little Rock through 1990s per AR Dept of Aeronautics historical records

note: JBR is currently EAS-dependent; Southern Airways Express serves STL and BNA

lostDestinations.source: KALB.com / search results: Delta Connection ceased Alexandria-Memphis route Nov 4, 2010; previously Northwest Airlink when NW had MEM hub
KALB.com Oct 2025: United suspended Houston service from AEX in June 2022 due to pilot shortage; SkyWest resumed May 2026
Alexandria airport history: Royale Airlines and other Louisiana regional carriers served MSY from AEX through 1980s-1990s
Search results: Delta flew B727-200/MD-80 to ATL/DFW/BHM from AEX in pre-2000 era per airport history

note: AEX moved to England Airpark (former England AFB) in 1992 from Esler Field. Currently served by AA to DFW, Delta to ATL; United Express to IAH resumed May 2026.

lostDestinations.carrier: Delta Connection (Express Airlines I / Northwest Airlink)
lostDestinations.source: Fort Smith airport history site flyfsm.com: Memphis service continued as Delta Connection until 2012 when Delta shut Memphis hub
flyfsm.com / search: Delta Connection added ATL nonstop 2007; all Delta service to FSM ended mid-2020 due to COVID-19
FSM airport search results: Frontier operated to OKC/Tulsa via B737 until 1984 shutdown
FSM history: Frontier Airlines served TUL until Frontier's shutdown; local carrier connections continued briefly
FSM search results: Braniff flew BAC-111 jets to multiple destinations including Chicago until 1979 deregulation exit

detail: Fort Smith once had multi-airline service including Braniff jets to Chicago and Frontier to Denver/OKC, reduced to single American Eagle DFW connection after Delta exited in 2020.
source: flyfsm.com/history; airport search results via Fort Smith Regional Airport history

lostDestinations.source: Drake Field history / search: American Eagle operated DFW and BNA nonstop from FYV in 1995; all shifted to XNA in 1998-1999
Drake Field 1995 schedule: American Eagle operated BNA nonstop; service moved to XNA Sept 1999
Drake Field 1995 schedule: NW Airlink operated MEM and FSM nonstop; shifted to XNA 1999
Drake Field 1995 schedule: TWA Express served STL and Springfield MO; all service moved to XNA 1999
Drake Field 1995 schedule: USAir Express served MCI and LIT; last airline US Airways moved to XNA Sept 1999

detail: Drake Field was the commercial airport for northwest Arkansas until Northwest Arkansas National Airport (XNA) opened in 1998; US Airways was the last carrier, moving to XNA in September 1999.
source: Drake Field airport history search results / XNA Encyclopedia of Arkansas entry

lostDestinations.source: TXK airport history / search: Delta/NW served TXK with Memphis hub connections through 1980s-early 1990s
Search results: Texas International served TXK with direct service to Houston; Continental briefly replaced TI routes post-merger 1982
TX Intl served multi-stop routes through TXK to MSY before deregulation; search results confirm TI operated Texarkana routes

detail: Texarkana Regional, an EAS-supported airport straddling Arkansas and Texas, retained only American Eagle DFW service after losing hub connections; 73,280 passengers in 2018 down significantly from 1980s peaks.
source: Texarkana Regional Airport history flytxk.com; Bureau of Transportation Statistics 2018 data

lastCarrier: American Eagle / Delta Connection / United Express

lostDestinations.source: SHV airport search results: Braniff flew BAC-111 jets to MSY and Fort Smith; Braniff shut down May 1982; Continental briefly served MSY routing
SHV history: Northwest Airlines flew DC-9 jets nonstop to MEM from SHV per airport history search results
SHV history search results: TWA served SHV with DC-9 jet service to STL
SHV search results: Braniff flew multi-stop routes to Kansas City, Chicago, Minneapolis from SHV before May 1982 shutdown
SHV Braniff routes included Kansas City via multi-stop B727 service until 1982 shutdown
SHV history: Continental operated DC-9 nonstop Houston from SHV through 1990s; service ended around 2000 per airport history

detail: Shreveport Regional was once a mini-hub for Delta Air Lines and served by Braniff, TWA, Continental, and Northwest; now reduced to American Eagle DFW, Delta Connection ATL, and United Express IAH.
source: flyshreveport.com history / airline service pages; multiple aviation history search results

lostDestinations.source: KNOE.com Nov 2021 / KLFY.com: United Airlines announced end of service at MLU effective January 3, 2022; operated 3 daily Houston flights
MLU history search results: Northwest Airlink flew BAe Jetstream/Saab 340 to Memphis; service ended with NW route restructuring mid-1990s
MLU history: L'Express operated Beechcraft turboprops to MSY hub; Royale Airlines flew MSY nonstop from MLU until 1989
MLU history: Delta operated B727/MD-80 jets to ATL/DFW/BHM from MLU; BHM route ended late 1990s per search results
MLU history: Royale Airlines served both MLU and AEX with connections to Houston/New Orleans; ceased 1989

detail: Monroe Regional, the birthplace of Delta Air Lines, lost United Airlines service (Houston) in January 2022, the last hub link beyond Atlanta and DFW, leaving Monroe with only 2 nonstop destinations.
source: KNOE.com 2021; WBRZ.com; Monroe Regional Airport About page flymonroe.org

lostDestinations.source: LCH history search results: Eastern Air Lines operated from LCH to Atlanta via Baton Rouge/New Orleans routing mid-1960s
LCH history: Eastern operated to MSY; Trans-Texas flew nonstop Monroe and direct New Orleans from LCH through late 1960s
LCH history: Trans-Texas flew direct to Memphis via Jackson MS routing from LCH until mid-1960s
LCH history search results: American Eagle served LCH with DFW and one-stop BNA via Gulfport through 1990s
LCH history: TX Intl flew direct service to San Antonio from LCH in 1961 per airport history search results

detail: Lake Charles Regional lost Eastern Air Lines and Trans-Texas multi-destination service by 1970; today retained only American Eagle to DFW and United Express to Houston as sole hub connections.
source: Lake Charles Regional Airport history search results / ifly.com guide

lostDestinations.source: HUM history search results: Gulf Coast Airlines 6 daily flights to MSY 1974-75; Royale Airlines continued MSY service through early 1980s
HUM history: Universal Airways operated direct flights to HOU via Patterson LA through 1979; Royale briefly served HOU routing

detail: Houma-Terrebonne Airport lost all scheduled passenger service by the mid-1980s when Royale Airlines ceased commuter operations; the airport now serves offshore oil industry helicopter operations and general aviation only.
source: Houma-Terrebonne Airport history search results / aviation history databases

lostDestinations.source: Acadiana Regional Airport Wikipedia: airport served by commuter airlines to MSY hub; Royale Airlines hub at MSY ceased 1989
Royale Airlines operated IAH hub routes from small Louisiana cities including New Iberia until airline ceased 1989

detail: Acadiana Regional Airport (New Iberia) lost all scheduled commercial service around 1989-1990 when Royale Airlines, which connected it to New Orleans and Houston hubs, ceased operations; airport operates as general aviation and helicopter training facility.
source: Acadiana Regional Airport Wikipedia; aviation history search results

lostDestinations.source: Hot Springs airport history / Stuttgart airport history: Trans-Texas DC-3 milk-run route Memphis-Helena-Stuttgart-Pine Bluff-LIT-HOT-Texarkana-Dallas operated 1953-1958/59
Same Trans-Texas DC-3 milk-run routing through Stuttgart; service ended late 1950s per airport history records

detail: Stuttgart Municipal Airport had scheduled service only via Trans-Texas Airways DC-3 milk-run route from 1953 to ~1959; the airport has operated as a general aviation facility ever since.
source: Hot Springs AR airport history site hotspringsar.gov; Stuttgart Municipal Airport Wikipedia
